content.claim: Hungary's church school takeover (increasing 60% attendance 2001–2011, 47% further 2011–2014) has substituted for network closure in small settlements, creating alternative institutional pathways with contested implications for parental choice and educational neutrality.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Polish system reform lens
limitations: Attendance increase does not measure whether it reflects parental choice, institutional monopoly, or both
No evidence on whether church takeover serves as substitute for planned closure or independent institutional shift
Contested normative framing ('violation of right to neutral education') reflects policy disagreement, not empirical fact about educational outcomes or parental satisfaction
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
